titleStmt.title: Gertrud Oppel
born Herischdorf, Silesia May 3, 1881; died Lugano, August 4, 1925

publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.p: Gertrud Oppel, was born Gertrud Bandelow in 1881 in the small Silesian town of Herischdorf, then part of Prussia, but which in 1945 fell to Poland, and was renamed Malinnik, now Jelenia Góra. Nothing of her life or upbringing is known before her marriage to Reinhard Oppel on May 15, 1911. Apparently she was a painter. She was the second of Oppel’s wives, his first being Anna Ernestine Catharina Brebeck (1884–1955), the couple having divorced on March 17, 1910.

With Reinhard, Gertrud had three children: Ulrich (b. June 2, 1910), Walter (b. August 10, 1913), and Hans Reinhard (b. May 14, 1915). Gertrud showed early signs of illness in January 1923, and was hospitalized in Kiel in January 1924, where she failed to improve; in October 1924 she was moved to Lugano in Switzerland, where she died in hospital on August 4, 1925, the cause of her death being unclear.

Gertrud figures frequently in Reinhard’s letters, particularly in 1923–26. During the later months of her illness, Hans Reinhard was in the care of Gertrud’s sister in Münster, while Ulrich and Walter remained in the family home in Kiel, in the care of a maid whenever Reinhard was in Leipzig. On the first anniversary of her death, he wrote: “A year ago today, I arrived in Lugano to see that no star was shining. And even until now I have not been at peace, my normal self; and I work only from a sense of duty, almost without any joy. Even now I am still always rebelling against fate.” (OJ 13/10, [36]). Reinhard composed an a capella Mass in her memory, which he tried unsuccessfully to persuade Furtwängler to perform.

div.head: Correspondence, Photography
div.p: No letters to Schenker, either from her or including her, survive among the extensive Oppel correspondence. One photograph of her is preserved: dated July 1917, it shows her seated in an armchair in a garden (OJ 72/11, item 2).

bibl.ref: Michael Heymel, Reinhard Oppel, in Lexikon verfolgter Musiker und Musikerinnen der NS-Zeit (LexM), Online-Lexikon des Musikwissenschaftlichen Instituts der Universität Hamburg
